description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on other facial features — illustrating selective visual attention.
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, concrete analogy for how attention mechanisms work: humans focus on high-resolution regions of interest while perceiving surroundings at lower resolution, directly motivating the mathematical attention concept.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of an attention lesson to build intuition before introducing any equations — ask students 'what are you looking at first and why?'
suggested_caption: Human visual attention: high-res focus regions vs. low-res periphery.

description: A sentence with attention weights visualized between words, showing how one word (e.g., 'eating') attends to other words in the same sentence with varying strengths indicated by shading or line thickness.
teaching_value: Bridges the gap between visual attention and linguistic attention, showing students that words in a sentence have differential relevance to each other — directly motivating self-attention and cross-attention in NLP models.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the visual attention dog example, before introducing seq2seq models, to extend the analogy from images to language.
suggested_caption: Words attend to other words with varying strength in a sentence.

description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing RNN encoder processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which the RNN decoder uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the bottleneck problem of seq2seq: all source information must be compressed into one fixed-length vector, making it clear why long sentences are problematic and motivating the need for attention.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the limitations of vanilla seq2seq before attention — ask students 'what happens to early words by the time the decoder finishes?'
suggested_caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: all source info compressed into one vector.

description: Detailed architecture diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention mechanism within an encoder-decoder model, showing bidirectional RNN encoder hidden states, alignment score computation, softmax over attention weights, weighted sum to form context vector, and decoder RNN receiving the context vector at each step.
teaching_value: Shows exactly how attention resolves the bottleneck: instead of one fixed vector, the decoder dynamically computes a weighted combination of all encoder hidden states at each decoding step, with the diagram making the data flow explicit.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck problem; walk through each component step-by-step — encoder states, score function, softmax, weighted sum, decoder input.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau additive attention: dynamic context from all encoder states. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)

description: Heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; cell brightness indicates attention weight, revealing which source words the model attends to when generating each target word.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that attention learns linguistically meaningful alignments (e.g., diagonal for monotonic translations, off-diagonal for reordered phrases), making abstract attention weights interpretable and validating the mechanism.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the attention weight formula to demonstrate what trained attention weights look like in practice — ask students to identify reordering patterns.
suggested_caption: Attention alignment matrix: French-to-English translation weights. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)

description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red and surrounding words are shaded in blue with varying intensity proportional to their attention activation level, showing intra-sentence dependencies.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates self-attention (intra-attention) by showing how a model relates a word to other positions in the same sequence, helping students understand why self-attention is useful for capturing long-range dependencies.
when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention as distinct from encoder-decoder attention — contrast with the sentence-level attention example to highlight that source and target are the same sequence.
suggested_caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to context words (blue intensity). (Cheng et al., 2016)

description: Image captioning example showing a photo of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with attention weight overlays highlighting which image regions the model focuses on when generating specific caption words, demonstrating soft attention over CNN feature maps.
teaching_value: Demonstrates soft attention applied to image captioning, showing students that attention is not limited to NLP — the model learns to focus on relevant image regions for each generated word, making attention weights spatially interpretable.
when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs. hard attention, or when demonstrating attention in computer vision tasks — ask students which image regions they expect the model to attend to for each word.
suggested_caption: Soft attention over image regions for caption generation. (Xu et al., 2015)

description: Side-by-side diagrams comparing global attention (left, attending to all source positions) and local attention (right, attending to a window of source positions around a predicted alignment point) in Luong et al.'s model.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the architectural difference between global and local attention, helping students understand the computational trade-off: global attention is more expressive but expensive; local attention is efficient but requires position prediction.
when_to_show: Show when teaching the Luong et al. attention variants after Bahdanau — use the side-by-side layout to directly compare scope of attention at each decoding step.
suggested_caption: Global vs. local attention: full sequence vs. windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)

description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing the controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) determining which memory rows to access.
teaching_value: Shows how attention mechanisms extend beyond sequence-to-sequence models to enable differentiable memory access in NTMs, helping students see attention as a general soft-selection mechanism applicable to external memory systems.
when_to_show: Show when extending attention concepts beyond NLP to memory-augmented networks — ask students how the attention vector over memory rows parallels attention over encoder hidden states.
suggested_caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller + attention-addressed external memory. (Graves et al., 2014)

description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over shift positions and a direct parametric distribution.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the design choice in NTM location-based addressing — how the shift kernel is parameterized affects the model's ability to perform relative position shifts over memory.
when_to_show: When explaining NTM location-based addressing, specifically after introducing the concept of convolutional shift and before discussing sharpening.
suggested_caption: Two parameterizations of NTM shift weighting distribution s_t.

description: Full flow diagram of the Neural Turing Machine addressing mechanism, showing the sequential steps: content-based addressing, interpolation with previous weights, location-based shift convolution, and sharpening to produce final attention weights w_t.
teaching_value: Provides a complete visual pipeline of how NTM generates attention weights, allowing students to see how content-based and location-based addressing are combined step-by-step.
when_to_show: Use as a summary diagram after explaining each individual NTM addressing step, or as an overview before diving into each component.
suggested_caption: NTM addressing pipeline: content, interpolation, shift, and sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)

description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence and a decoder RNN using attention over encoder hidden states to output pointers (indices) back into the input sequence rather than fixed vocabulary tokens.
teaching_value: Illustrates how Pointer Networks differ from standard seq2seq by using attention scores directly as output probabilities pointing to input positions, making the output vocabulary dynamic and input-dependent.
when_to_show: When introducing Pointer Networks as a solution to problems where output elements are positions in the input (e.g., sorting, TSP), contrasting with standard seq2seq attention.
suggested_caption: Pointer Network: decoder attention directly selects input positions. (Vinyals et al., 2015)

description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, softmax, and scaling; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, linear projections, concatenation, and final linear layer.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how multi-head attention runs multiple attention operations in parallel on projected subspaces, then combines them — helping students understand why it captures richer representations than single-head attention.
when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer's core attention mechanism; show left panel first for single-head attention, then right panel to explain multi-head extension.
suggested_caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, both with residual connections and layer normalization.
teaching_value: Shows students the modular structure of the Transformer encoder, clarifying how positional encoding, self-attention, residual connections, and feed-forward layers are composed into a stack.
when_to_show: When explaining the Transformer encoder architecture; use to walk through each sub-layer and the role of residual connections and normalization.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder: self-attention + feed-forward layers with residuals. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of the Transformer decoder stack showing: output embeddings with positional encoding, masked multi-head self-attention, encoder-decoder cross-attention, feed-forward layers, residual connections, layer normalization, and final linear+softmax output.
teaching_value: Helps students understand how the decoder differs from the encoder by incorporating masked self-attention (to prevent future token leakage) and cross-attention over encoder outputs.
when_to_show: After explaining the encoder; highlight the masked attention layer and cross-attention to encoder, contrasting with the encoder's structure.
suggested_caption: Transformer decoder with masked self-attention and cross-attention layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Complete Transformer architecture diagram showing the full encoder (left) and decoder (right) stacks side by side, with data flow from input/output embeddings through all layers to the final softmax output probabilities.
teaching_value: Provides the complete picture of the Transformer model, allowing students to see how encoder and decoder interact and trace the full information flow from input tokens to output probabilities.
when_to_show: Use as a capstone diagram after explaining encoder and decoder separately, or as an overview at the start of a Transformer lesson to orient students.
suggested_caption: Full Transformer architecture: encoder-decoder with multi-head attention. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) architecture diagram showing alternating temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and attention layers (for selective retrieval), used for meta-learning tasks.
teaching_value: Illustrates how SNAIL combines causal convolutions for positional awareness with attention for long-range dependency, addressing the Transformer's weakness in positional sensitivity for RL/meta-learning.
when_to_show: When discussing limitations of the Transformer for sequential/RL tasks and how SNAIL addresses them by combining convolution and attention.
suggested_caption: SNAIL: interleaved temporal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)

description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the receptive field of a convolutional operation (small local region) versus self-attention (entire image/sequence), illustrating the difference in context access.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students why self-attention is more powerful than convolution for capturing long-range dependencies — convolution is limited to local neighborhoods while self-attention attends globally.
when_to_show: When motivating the use of self-attention in vision models (SAGAN); use to contrast local convolutional receptive fields with global attention reach.
suggested_caption: Convolution accesses local regions; self-attention accesses the entire image.

description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing image feature maps branched into three 1x1 convolution paths (f, g, h corresponding to query, key, value), attention map computation via softmax, and weighted sum to produce self-attention feature maps added back to input.
teaching_value: Shows students how the Transformer's Q/K/V attention framework is adapted to 2D image feature maps using 1x1 convolutions, enabling spatial self-attention in GANs.
when_to_show: When teaching how attention mechanisms transfer from NLP to computer vision; use to show the direct analogy between Transformer attention and SAGAN's spatial attention.
suggested_caption: SAGAN self-attention: Q/K/V via 1x1 convolutions over image features. (Zhang et al., 2018)

description: A grid of 128×128 generated images from the Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) model, showing diverse object classes such as dogs, birds, and other animals with fine-grained details and coherent global structure.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical output quality of SAGAN, showing students how self-attention mechanisms in GANs enable the model to capture long-range spatial dependencies and generate globally consistent, high-resolution images — something local convolutions alone struggle to achieve.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention formulation (attention map α_ij, 1x1 conv filters W_f/W_g/W_h) to give students a concrete visual payoff — use it to ask 'which parts of the image do you think the attention map focuses on for a dog's eye?'
suggested_caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showing self-attention-enabled global coherence. (Zhang et al., 2018)

description: High-level diagram of the Transformer architecture showing the encoding component (stack of encoders) and decoding component (stack of decoders) with connections between them, resembling an 'opened up' black box view.
teaching_value: Gives students their first internal view of the Transformer, establishing the two major components (encoder stack and decoder stack) and their relationship before diving into details.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the Transformer as a black box, to transition students from the abstract input/output view to the internal component structure.
suggested_caption: Transformer internals: encoder stack and decoder stack with connections. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram showing the Transformer as a black box for machine translation, with a source-language sentence as input and a target-language translation as output, labeled with 'Encoders' and 'Decoders' blocks.
teaching_value: Anchors the abstract architecture in a concrete use case (machine translation), helping students understand the purpose of the model before learning its internals.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a Transformer introduction to motivate the architecture with a real-world task before revealing internal components.
suggested_caption: Transformer as a black box: input sentence in, translated sentence out. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram illustrating the stacked encoder and decoder architecture, showing six encoder layers stacked vertically on the left and six decoder layers stacked vertically on the right, with arrows indicating data flow between them.
teaching_value: Makes the concept of stacking identical encoder/decoder layers concrete and visual, helping students understand depth in the Transformer and how information flows through multiple layers.
when_to_show: Show when explaining that the Transformer uses multiple identical encoder and decoder layers, to reinforce the stacking concept and the number six from the original paper.
suggested_caption: Six stacked encoders (left) and six stacked decoders (right) in the Transformer. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram of a single Transformer encoder block showing two sub-layers: a Self-Attention layer at the bottom and a Feed-Forward Neural Network layer on top, with arrows showing data flow upward through both layers.
teaching_value: Clearly decomposes the encoder into its two sub-layers, giving students a precise mental model of what happens inside each encoder before exploring either sub-layer in depth.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the internal structure of a single encoder, before explaining self-attention or feed-forward networks in detail.
suggested_caption: Single encoder: self-attention layer feeds into a feed-forward network. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram of a single Transformer decoder block showing three sub-layers: Self-Attention, Encoder-Decoder Attention (cross-attention), and a Feed-Forward Neural Network, with arrows indicating data flow including input from the encoder stack.
teaching_value: Highlights the key structural difference between encoder and decoder — the additional cross-attention sub-layer — helping students understand how the decoder attends to encoder outputs.
when_to_show: Show when contrasting the decoder structure with the encoder, specifically to introduce the cross-attention mechanism that connects encoder and decoder.
suggested_caption: Single decoder: self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward sub-layers. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram showing word embedding conversion: input words (e.g., 'You', 'are', 'welcome') are each converted into 512-dimensional embedding vectors represented as colored bars, which then enter the bottom encoder.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the first step of Transformer processing — converting discrete tokens into continuous vector representations — and establishes the 512-dimensional vector size used throughout the model.
when_to_show: Show when introducing how raw text is prepared for the Transformer, before explaining how vectors flow through encoder layers.
suggested_caption: Each input word is converted to a 512-dimensional embedding vector. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram showing multiple word embedding vectors (one per input word) flowing in parallel through the self-attention layer and then the feed-forward layer of a single encoder, with separate paths for each word position.
teaching_value: Visualizes the key property that each word has its own path through the encoder, and introduces the distinction between the self-attention layer (where paths interact) and the feed-forward layer (where paths are independent and parallelizable).
when_to_show: Show when explaining how multiple tokens are processed simultaneously in the encoder, and to set up the distinction between self-attention (has cross-position dependencies) and feed-forward (fully parallel).
suggested_caption: Each word flows its own path through the encoder; self-attention creates cross-path dependencies. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram showing a short example sentence being processed through the encoder, with vectors for each word position flowing through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers, used to set up the detailed self-attention explanation.
teaching_value: Provides a concrete, simplified example sentence to ground the upcoming detailed explanation of self-attention, making the abstract mechanism easier to follow.
when_to_show: Show just before diving into the step-by-step self-attention calculation, to establish the example sentence that will be used throughout the detailed walkthrough.
suggested_caption: A short example sentence entering the encoder, preparing for self-attention walkthrough. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Visualization of self-attention in action: the word 'it' in a sentence is highlighted, with attention lines connecting it to other words (especially 'animal'), showing which words the model attends to when encoding 'it'.
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, interpretable example of what self-attention computes — showing that the model learns to associate pronouns with their referents — making the abstract mechanism tangible.
when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention at a high level, before the mathematical details, to give students an intuitive understanding of what the mechanism achieves.
suggested_caption: Self-attention: 'it' attends strongly to 'animal' to resolve coreference. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram illustrating the creation of Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors for each input word embedding by multiplying with three learned weight matrices (WQ, WK, WV), showing the dimensional reduction from 512 to 64.
teaching_value: Explains the foundational step of self-attention — projecting embeddings into Q/K/V spaces — and clarifies the purpose and dimensionality of each vector type, which is essential for understanding the attention score calculation.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the detailed self-attention explanation, as the first step in the Q/K/V computation walkthrough, before showing how scores are calculated.
suggested_caption: Each word embedding is projected into Query, Key, and Value vectors (dim=64). (Alammar, 2018)

description: Step-by-step diagram showing how self-attention scores are computed for the word 'Thinking' by taking dot products of its query vector (q1) with the key vectors (k1, k2) of each word in the sequence, producing raw attention scores.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the dot-product scoring mechanism, showing students exactly which vectors interact and why — making the abstract formula q·k tangible with labeled arrows and example words.
when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the scoring step of self-attention, before discussing scaling or softmax, to ground the dot-product formula in a concrete word-level example.
suggested_caption: Self-attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Alammar)

description: Diagram extending the self-attention score computation to show division by √64 (scaling step), followed by a softmax operation that normalizes scores into a probability distribution, and then multiplication of each value vector by its softmax weight.
teaching_value: Visually connects three sequential steps — scaling, softmax normalization, and value weighting — helping students understand why scaling stabilizes gradients and how softmax converts raw scores into interpretable attention weights.
when_to_show: Show after the dot-product scoring diagram, when explaining why scores are scaled and how softmax produces a valid probability distribution over positions.
suggested_caption: Scaling by √d_k and softmax normalization of attention scores. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the final steps of self-attention: weighted value vectors (each value vector multiplied by its softmax score) are summed to produce the output vector z1 for the first word position.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how the weighted sum aggregates information from all positions into a single output vector, making the 'soft lookup' intuition concrete — high-attention words contribute more to the output.
when_to_show: Show after the softmax diagram to complete the full self-attention pipeline, emphasizing how the final output vector encodes context from the entire sequence.
suggested_caption: Weighted sum of value vectors produces self-attention output z1. (Alammar)

description: Matrix-form diagram showing input embedding matrix X being multiplied by trained weight matrices W^Q, W^K, and W^V to simultaneously produce the Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) matrices for all positions at once.
teaching_value: Bridges the word-level intuition to the efficient matrix implementation, showing students how batching all positions into a single matrix multiplication replaces the per-word vector operations described earlier.
when_to_show: Show after the step-by-step word-level diagrams, when transitioning to the practical matrix implementation to explain how transformers achieve computational efficiency.
suggested_caption: Input matrix X multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V to form Q, K, V. (Alammar)

description: Single condensed formula diagram showing the complete self-attention matrix calculation: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k)V, combining all six steps into one matrix expression.
teaching_value: Presents the canonical self-attention formula in visual form, helping students connect the step-by-step process to the compact mathematical expression used in the original paper.
when_to_show: Show after the Q/K/V matrix construction diagram as the culminating summary, and revisit when students encounter the formula in the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper.
suggested_caption: Full self-attention formula: softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V in matrix form. (Alammar)

description: Diagram illustrating multi-headed attention by showing the same input being processed through eight parallel sets of Q/K/V weight matrices, each producing a separate Z matrix (Z0 through Z7).
teaching_value: Makes the parallelism of multi-head attention visually explicit, helping students understand that multiple independent attention computations run simultaneously, each potentially capturing different relationships.
when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-headed attention, after students understand single-head self-attention, to illustrate why eight separate computations are performed and what they each produce.
suggested_caption: Eight parallel attention heads each produce a separate Z matrix. (Alammar)

description: Visualization showing eight separate Z output matrices (Z0–Z7) from eight attention heads, highlighting the challenge of having multiple output matrices when the feed-forward layer expects a single matrix.
teaching_value: Motivates the need for the concatenation and projection step by making the dimensionality mismatch problem concrete — students see why eight matrices must be combined before passing to the next layer.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the multi-head QKV diagram to pose the 'how do we combine these?' problem before revealing the W^O projection solution.
suggested_caption: Eight attention head outputs Z0–Z7 must be condensed for the next layer. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the concatenation of all eight Z matrices followed by multiplication by weight matrix W^O to produce a single combined output matrix, completing the multi-headed attention mechanism.
teaching_value: Shows the concrete solution to combining multiple attention heads — concatenation followed by a learned linear projection — teaching students how the model learns to integrate information from all representation subspaces.
when_to_show: Show after presenting the eight Z matrices problem, as the resolution step, and emphasize that W^O is also a learned parameter trained end-to-end.
suggested_caption: Concatenated attention heads projected by W^O into single output matrix. (Alammar)

description: Comprehensive recap diagram showing the entire multi-headed self-attention pipeline in one view: input embeddings → multiple Q/K/V projections → parallel attention computations → concatenation → W^O projection → final output.
teaching_value: Provides a unified visual summary of all multi-head attention components together, helping students consolidate the full pipeline and see how all the pieces (weight matrices, heads, projections) fit together.
when_to_show: Show as a consolidation diagram after explaining each individual step, or use as a reference when students need to review the complete multi-head attention mechanism.
suggested_caption: Complete multi-headed self-attention pipeline from input to output. (Alammar)

description: Attention visualization showing which tokens different attention heads focus on when encoding the word 'it' in the sentence 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired', with colored lines indicating attention weights to other words.
teaching_value: Provides an interpretability example showing that different attention heads learn to resolve different linguistic relationships (e.g., coreference), making the abstract concept of 'representation subspaces' concrete and meaningful.
when_to_show: Show after explaining multi-headed attention mechanics to demonstrate real-world utility — use it to motivate why multiple heads are beneficial by showing they capture different linguistic phenomena.
suggested_caption: Attention heads reveal which words 'it' attends to during encoding. (Alammar)

description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to various other words, making the combined picture cluttered and hard to interpret.
teaching_value: Demonstrates why visualizing all attention heads at once is cognitively overwhelming, motivating the need to examine heads individually and understand that each head captures different relationships.
when_to_show: After showing individual attention head visualizations, use this to illustrate the complexity of multi-head attention and prompt discussion about how different heads specialize.
suggested_caption: All attention heads combined for 'it' — hard to interpret. (Alammar, The Illustrated Transformer)

description: Diagram showing positional encoding vectors being added to word embedding vectors before they enter the encoder. Each word's embedding is shown as a colored vector, with a corresponding positional encoding vector added to it, illustrated with a 4-dimensional example.
teaching_value: Concretely shows how positional information is injected into the model by vector addition, making abstract positional encoding tangible with a low-dimensional (4D) example before scaling up.
when_to_show: When introducing positional encoding; use this before showing the full 512-dimensional heatmap to build intuition with a simple 4D example.
suggested_caption: Positional encoding vectors added to word embeddings (4D example). (Alammar)

description: Small heatmap visualization of positional encoding values for a short sequence with 4-dimensional embeddings. Each row represents a word position and each column a dimension, color-coded to show values between -1 and 1.
teaching_value: Provides a concrete, small-scale visual of what positional encoding values look like, helping students see the pattern before examining the full 512-dimension version.
when_to_show: Immediately after explaining that positional encodings are added to embeddings; use as a stepping stone before the large 512-dimension heatmap.
suggested_caption: Positional encoding pattern for 4-dimensional embeddings. (Alammar)

description: Large heatmap showing positional encodings for many sequence positions (rows) across 512 dimensions (columns). Color-coded from -1 to 1, revealing sinusoidal wave patterns that vary in frequency across dimensions.
teaching_value: Reveals the sinusoidal structure of positional encodings at scale — students can see how lower dimensions oscillate faster and higher dimensions slower, explaining how the model distinguishes positions.
when_to_show: After the 4D example; use to show the full-scale pattern and discuss how the sinusoidal structure allows generalization to unseen sequence lengths.
suggested_caption: 512-dimensional positional encoding heatmap — sinusoidal patterns visible. (Alammar)

description: Heatmap of positional encodings from the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper, showing the interleaved sine/cosine encoding pattern across positions and dimensions, slightly different from the Tensor2Tensor implementation.
teaching_value: Shows the original paper's positional encoding visualization, allowing students to compare the interleaved sine/cosine method with the Tensor2Tensor concatenation approach and understand implementation variations.
when_to_show: When discussing the original paper's positional encoding formula; use alongside the Tensor2Tensor version to highlight that multiple valid implementations exist.
suggested_caption: Original paper's interleaved sine/cosine positional encoding heatmap. (Vaswani et al., via Alammar)

description: Diagram of a single encoder layer showing the residual (skip) connection and layer normalization step. The input vector is shown bypassing the self-attention sub-layer and being added back to the sub-layer's output before layer norm is applied.
teaching_value: Visually explains the Add & Norm step in the Transformer encoder, showing how residual connections preserve gradient flow and how layer normalization stabilizes training.
when_to_show: When explaining encoder sub-layer structure; use to show why residual connections matter for training deep networks and how they are implemented in the Transformer.
suggested_caption: Residual connection and layer normalization in a Transformer encoder layer. (Alammar)

description: Diagram of a stacked Transformer with 2 encoders and 2 decoders, each sub-layer showing residual connections and layer normalization. Arrows show data flow through the stack, illustrating how both encoder and decoder stacks apply Add & Norm.
teaching_value: Shows the full stacked architecture with residual connections and layer norm at every sub-layer in both encoder and decoder, helping students understand the repeated modular structure of the Transformer.
when_to_show: After explaining individual encoder residuals; use to generalize the pattern to the full encoder-decoder stack and reinforce the modular, repeated structure.
suggested_caption: 2-layer Transformer stack with residual connections and layer norm shown. (Alammar)

description: Detailed diagram of a single encoder sub-layer showing the self-attention mechanism with residual connection and layer normalization. Input vectors flow into self-attention, the output is added to the original input (residual), then layer norm is applied.
teaching_value: Provides a step-by-step visual of the Add & Norm operation specifically for the self-attention sub-layer, making the data flow concrete and showing exactly where residuals and normalization occur.
when_to_show: When students need a detailed view of the encoder sub-layer; use to trace the exact path of a vector through self-attention, addition, and normalization.
suggested_caption: Self-attention sub-layer with residual connection and layer normalization. (Alammar)

description: Animated GIF showing the first decoding step of a Transformer. The encoder processes the full input sequence, then the decoder begins generating output one token at a time, with the first output token being produced.
teaching_value: Animates the autoregressive decoding process, showing students how the decoder starts from the encoder's output and generates tokens sequentially, making the temporal nature of decoding clear.
when_to_show: When introducing the decoder side; use this animation first to show the overall decoding process before explaining masked self-attention and encoder-decoder attention in detail.
suggested_caption: Transformer decoder generating the first output token autoregressively. (Alammar)

description: Animated GIF showing multiple decoding steps of a Transformer, with the decoder generating successive output tokens. Illustrates how each new token is fed back as input to the decoder and how attention vectors K and V from the encoder are reused at each step.
teaching_value: Shows the full autoregressive loop — each generated token becomes the next decoder input — and how encoder K/V vectors are reused across all decoding steps, clarifying the encoder-decoder attention mechanism.
when_to_show: After the first decoding step animation; use to show the full multi-step generation process and explain how encoder outputs are reused and why decoder self-attention must be masked.
suggested_caption: Transformer decoder generating output tokens step-by-step using encoder K/V. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the final stages of the Transformer decoder: a logits vector of 10,000 cells (one per vocabulary word) produced by the Linear layer, followed by a Softmax layer that converts scores to probabilities, with the highest-probability cell selected as the output word.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how raw decoder outputs (logits) are mapped to a probability distribution over the vocabulary and how a single output word is selected, bridging abstract math to the actual word-generation mechanism.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how the Transformer generates output tokens — specifically when transitioning from internal representations to actual word predictions.
suggested_caption: Linear layer produces logits; softmax converts them to word probabilities. (Alammar)

description: A one-hot encoding vector example for a small vocabulary, showing how the word 'am' is represented as a vector with a single 1 in the position corresponding to 'am' and 0s elsewhere.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the one-hot encoding scheme used to represent discrete vocabulary words as vectors, which is foundational for understanding how target labels are formatted during training and how output probabilities are compared to ground truth.
when_to_show: Show when introducing how words are represented as vectors during training, before discussing loss functions or comparing model outputs to target labels.
suggested_caption: One-hot encoding represents each vocabulary word as a sparse vector. (Alammar)

description: A one-hot encoded vector over a six-word vocabulary ('a', 'am', 'i', 'thanks', 'student', '<eos>'), visually showing which position is active (1) for a specific word, used as the target label when training to translate 'merci' into 'thanks'.
teaching_value: Provides a minimal, concrete example of one-hot encoding with a toy vocabulary, making it easy to understand how target outputs are structured and how the model's predicted probability distribution should ideally match this sparse vector.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the training objective — specifically when illustrating what the 'correct answer' looks like in vector form before introducing the loss function.
suggested_caption: Six-word vocabulary one-hot target for translating 'merci' → 'thanks'. (Alammar)

description: Side-by-side comparison of the model's output probability distribution (spread across vocabulary words) versus the target one-hot label distribution (all probability on 'thanks') for the single-word translation example 'merci' → 'thanks', illustrating the discrepancy the loss function must minimize.
teaching_value: Visually shows the gap between an untrained model's diffuse output distribution and the sharp target distribution, making the purpose of the loss function (cross-entropy) intuitive and concrete.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the loss function — use it to motivate why cross-entropy is needed and what 'minimizing loss' means geometrically.
suggested_caption: Model output vs. target distribution: loss measures their difference. (Alammar)

description: Multiple probability distribution panels showing what the model should output at each decoding step for the translation 'je suis étudiant' → 'i am a student', with each panel representing one time step and the target word having high probability.
teaching_value: Extends the single-word example to a full sentence, showing how the training objective applies across multiple sequential decoding steps and reinforcing that the model must learn to produce correct distributions at every position.
when_to_show: Show after the single-word loss example to generalize the concept to multi-step sequence generation and explain how loss is accumulated across all output positions.
suggested_caption: Target probability distributions across all decoding steps for a sentence. (Alammar)

description: Probability distribution panels showing what a well-trained Transformer model actually produces at each decoding step for 'je suis étudiant' → 'i am a student', with near-certain probability mass on the correct word at each step.
teaching_value: Shows the ideal end-state of training — sharp, confident distributions on correct words — helping students understand what convergence looks like and contrasting it with the untrained model's diffuse distributions.
when_to_show: Show after discussing the loss function and training process, as a 'goal state' visualization, and use it to introduce greedy vs. beam search decoding strategies.
suggested_caption: Trained model produces near-certain distributions on correct output words. (Alammar)

description: A word-sequence attention illustration: one query vector is compared (via dot products) against key vectors for each word to produce attention weights (softmax implied), which are then used to compute a weighted average of the corresponding value vectors, yielding an attended output representation.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract Q–K similarity and weighted-sum over V concrete, helping students connect the math (scores → softmax weights → weighted average) to the intuitive idea of focusing on the most relevant tokens.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the attention equation to map each term (Q, K, V, scores, weights, output) to a visual step-by-step pipeline.
suggested_caption: Attention over words: dot(Q,K) → weights → weighted sum of V (UvA DLC).

description: Block diagram of scaled dot-product attention: inputs Q, K, V flow through a matrix multiplication QKᵀ, scaling by 1/sqrt(dk), optional masking, softmax to obtain attention probabilities, and a final multiplication by V to produce the attention output.
teaching_value: Clarifies the exact computation order and where scaling and masking occur, which is crucial for implementing self-attention correctly and understanding numerical stability and causal/padding constraints.
when_to_show: Show when students move from the intuitive attention idea to the efficient matrix-form implementation, especially before coding QKᵀ/softmax/V steps.
suggested_caption: Scaled dot-product attention computation graph (UvA DLC).

description: Multi-head attention schematic: Q, K, V are linearly projected into multiple heads (separate subspaces), each head runs scaled dot-product attention independently, the head outputs are concatenated, and a final linear projection produces the layer output.
teaching_value: Demonstrates why multiple heads are more expressive than a single attention map by showing parallel attention computations over different learned subspaces and the concat+projection recombination.
when_to_show: Show after students understand single-head attention, to motivate multiple heads and to explain tensor shapes (split into heads, then concat).
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention: parallel heads then concat and project (UvA DLC).

description: Transformer encoder architecture diagram: token embeddings combined with positional encodings feed stacked encoder blocks containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, each wrapped with residual connections and layer normalization.
teaching_value: Provides the big-picture wiring of a Transformer encoder, helping students place self-attention within the full block (residuals, normalization, FFN) and understand what repeats across layers.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from attention as an operation to the full Transformer layer/stack, or when debugging why residuals/norm are needed for stable training.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder block with self-attention and FFN (UvA DLC).

description: Plot illustrating sinusoidal positional encoding across positions and hidden dimensions, showing alternating sine/cosine components with different wavelengths depending on the dimension index.
teaching_value: Helps students see how position information is injected without recurrence by visualizing periodic patterns and how different dimensions encode different scales of positional variation.
when_to_show: Show right after presenting the PE(pos,i) formula to connect the equation to an interpretable signal pattern across dimensions.
suggested_caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding patterns across dimensions (UvA DLC).

description: Heatmap visualization of positional encodings with position on one axis and hidden dimension on the other; color indicates the added value per (pos, dim), revealing phase shifts and increasing wavelengths across dimensions.
teaching_value: Makes the structure of positional encodings tangible: students can identify phase offsets between adjacent dimensions and how frequency decreases with dimension, supporting intuition about relative-position linearity.
when_to_show: Show when explaining why different dimensions use different wavelengths and how nearby dimensions relate (e.g., dim 1 vs 2 phase shift, dim 2 vs 3 longer period).
suggested_caption: Heatmap of sinusoidal positional encodings over position and dimension (UvA DLC).

description: Learning-rate schedule plot for Transformer warm-up: learning rate increases during an initial warm-up phase and then decays afterward according to a predefined schedule.
teaching_value: Connects training stability issues to a concrete optimization practice, helping students recognize and reproduce the warm-up+decay schedule commonly used for training Transformers.
when_to_show: Show when students encounter unstable early training or are configuring an optimizer; use it to justify warm-up steps and how to choose warm-up length.
suggested_caption: Transformer learning-rate warm-up followed by decay (UvA DLC).

description: Grid of attention maps for a sequence-to-sequence task: rows correspond to Transformer layers and columns to attention heads; each subplot is a SeqLen×SeqLen matrix showing where each token attends, with strong mass near the flipped-index target and some spread to nearby positions.
teaching_value: Teaches how to interpret attention tensors ([layers, heads, query positions, key positions]) and diagnose learned behavior by visually verifying that heads/layers capture the intended alignment pattern.
when_to_show: Show after training a toy task to validate whether the model learned the desired mapping; use it to discuss head diversity and layer-wise refinement.
suggested_caption: Attention maps by layer and head reveal learned token alignment (UvA DLC).

description: Example set for anomaly detection built from CIFAR-100 images: a small collection of images where most belong to the same class and one image is from a different class (the anomaly), illustrating the unordered-set input format.
teaching_value: Grounds the abstract 'set anomaly detection' task in a concrete input/output example, clarifying what constitutes an anomaly and why permutation-invariant/equivariant models are appropriate.
when_to_show: Show before introducing the dataset sampling procedure or model, to ensure students understand the task definition and what the model must detect.
suggested_caption: CIFAR-100 set with one anomalous image among same-class images (UvA DLC).

description: Grid of attention heatmaps from a Transformer used for set anomaly detection, organized by layer and head (e.g., Layer 1–4, Head 1–4). Each heatmap shows attention probabilities between the 10 set elements (9 similar images and one outlier), with several heads placing strong attention on the last element (the anomaly candidate).
teaching_value: Helps students interpret multi-head self-attention by linking specific heads/layers to model behavior: which items are treated as salient, how information is routed across the set, and how attention concentration can reveal the detected outlier.
when_to_show: After students understand self-attention math, show this to practice reading attention matrices and to ask: which heads attend to the suspected outlier and why might different layers specialize differently?
suggested_caption: Attention maps by layer/head highlighting the outlier item (UvA Deep Learning Course).

description: Second grid of Transformer attention heatmaps for the same set anomaly detection task, showing contrasting head behaviors: some heads strongly attend to the last image (outlier), while later-layer heads assign very low attention to it; other heads distribute attention more evenly, resembling a weighted average over all set elements.
teaching_value: Demonstrates that different heads can implement different roles (outlier focusing vs. averaging/context aggregation) and that later layers may suppress attention to an anomalous element, reinforcing how attention patterns relate to the model’s anomaly decision and potential failure cases.
when_to_show: Use when discussing interpretability or debugging: have learners compare heads/layers, identify averaging vs. outlier-focused heads, and predict when the model might fail if attention does not isolate the anomaly.
suggested_caption: Layer/head attention patterns: outlier-focused vs. averaging heads (UvA Deep Learning Course).
